speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And another thing, I put myself into a perspective. Right. I am [INAUDIBLE], and I'm not afraid. You see, wherever I went, I was confident, smiling. So you see, you-- you-- sort of you draw danger towards you. So it all depends what sort of an attitude you take. But I was so convinced, you know, I am what I am. Sort of-- it never came to them. I mean, I can sometimes [? feel ?] I tamed the wildest one of them. Just sort of came my way. And so it was quite handy because I-- I could get food, or everything which was available to mother and to Frank. And I freely went into that [INAUDIBLE]. I had my friend. But they know-- they knew knew who I am, what I am. And the caretaker knew exactly. So when I got into that [INAUDIBLE], I put the-- put the star on. So I'm one of them. And when I left, I would take the star off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you not have been allowed in, if you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everything. I was still free. You could-- I mean, still Gentiles lived in that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in-- in in places. So it wasn't all really Jewish. Ghetto became only the very last months of the-- of the occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It only was sort of a designated living area for--

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did you feel that you had to put the star on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, to put the-- I had to because that was-- when I'm in the house, I'm Jewish. I mean, people knew me there. And the caretaker knew me. I mean, I didn't tell them that-- I just don't live there, but I'm still Jewish. Right? I could be there because that's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was communicating. And even, I stayed sometimes overnight. You know, I felt good with them. Mother, Frank, together. So it was-- life was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] once-- and it's this occasion that I went to-- to visit and to [? bring ?] something for mother and Frank, I stayed there. And they came and they circled the whole area. And they wanted to-- sort of to identify everyone who lived in that area. Where-- what are they? Who are they? And then, I was me because there was no point of denying.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the able-bodied people who were there, men up to 60 and-- and from 18 years old, they gathered them and they marched us and took us for some work in-- in [INAUDIBLE]. It was a-- a military airport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So imagine, I was caught. But-- so we had been led there. And we stayed there for two nights and two days. I mean, there's nothing. They put us in a hangar. And-- but we didn't do anything. And some decision came to bring us back to-- to Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think Frank must have heard what's happening, so he was staying in a sort of a street where we were in passing. And I didn't recognize Frank on the foot path. He had eye contact. And then he said, step out. He doesn't look. You know, the guard. So I stepped out [? to this thing. ?] I was back again. So there I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's luck. It's-- it's fate. I don't know what it is, but fate or luck was playing a big part in my survival. So everything went back further. And-- Frank has obtained some Swiss or Swedish-- yes, Swedish passport or-- or paper which-- that entitled them to go to a Swedish protected house in [INAUDIBLE], which was-- that was this entirely new area there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them? For him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: For him and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mother. And if I wanted to. There was quite a few of them. So if I wanted to. So they moved on from there and went into that protected house. That was a little bit more difficult because-- for me, too, and for them because it was very isolated. And they are in one house and very exposed. You see, when you live in a big thing, you have more-- much more chance of either to sneak away. [? But ?] but he seemed very protective and very. So there I had to do quite a lot of looking after, which I had because the food was scarce and winter started to come. And I visited them quite frequently. And then, more and more it became more difficult or dangerous. And as I was among these police and everything in the-- in the restaurant, I heard all the news coming. And one evening, you know, people were talking, two policemen. And they said that there's going to be a big night. They're going to-- to drag the Jews out of these-- of these protected houses. And they're shooting them into the Danube. I mean, that's what I heard. I've got to-- got to do something. I went to my sister and I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was quite late. And I think that was beginning of December or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 1944. So I told Edith and I had a plan. I say, Edith, I'm going to see Mother and Frank. And I think that what is happening. I don't know what I'm going to do with them, but they have to come to your place if they come. Because I wanted to make some arrangement where I live, but I had to talk first to the next door-- to people who I shared a flat with-- that I found a good housekeeper for them. Because they were all looking for a good housekeeper. She's an excellent cook. She's beautiful. She's clean. She's-- but one thing, she has a child out of wedlock. Frank did not have papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, that's [INAUDIBLE]. So Frank is a bastard. But when you come to save, it's everything [INAUDIBLE]. So Edith was a bit hesitant because I can understand, you know, she wasn't on her own. There was four people's life at-- at risk where Edith lived. See, there all strangers. She was the only one. But she had no choice. She had to agree to it, right? So I got dressed shabbily and I went to-- I went to that house in-- that protected house, and-- where my mother and Frank stayed. That-- no soul in the street. It was dusk, the evening. And nobody was walking. And there's no life in it. Or-- and-- so I just couldn't go in. So I had to knock on the door. Somebody came in and they opened the door. I said, what-- I would like to see-- I would like to talk to Mrs. Lowy. To my mother, right? So she called my mother. I couldn't get into the house because I wasn't Jewish. So Mother came down. And I said in sort of briefly, but very strongly. I said, Mother. Now you listen to me and do exactly what I am telling you. As soon as it get dark, take off your yellow star from you and from Frank. Walk out. And go to-- put yourself on a-- on a-- on a tram, and I'll meet you at Edith's place. But how can I--? Just do it. Because I said, there's no-- there's no option. I mean, there's no other way. She had to leave that house. So-- Would they have-- would they have found her in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would they have found her in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they-- they'd just empty the house and take everybody out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Did she take anything with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, wait a second. Then she said, but I would like to take something with me. I can't go without anything. Just a little briefcase. Frank needs-- And the more time I spend there, the more dangerous the situation became. But I could not convince my mother. So she went up. There was a little, nice suitcase. She packed a few essential. I mean, everybody needs something somewhere. She gave it to me. And I took it, and I started to walk. And there was nobody around. And when I walk, I hear steps behind me. I walk a little bit faster. More steps.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, somebody's following me. I've got to stop. Because what's the point of running? So I stopped and look behind. I said, do you want something from me? In Hungarian, of course. [INAUDIBLE] looked at me. Said, what have you been doing in the Jewish house? I said, what Jewish house? Are you Jewish too? I said, what's Jewish? Everything sort of denied. So [INAUDIBLE]. I mean, I was in Russia. I was a refugee from there. I said, [INAUDIBLE] heard anything like that. I don't know what it is. So he already softened down. It was a police, or-- a secret police, or what have you, who was protecting that area. So I said, anyhow. We will find out everything. But he was leading me to [INAUDIBLE] 60, which was the headquarters of this-- the police. I mean, whoever went in there, especially men, Jewish men, never came out alive. I mean, the identification was quite easy. And so he gave me a lecture about Jews. They all have to be murdered, killed. They are pests and, you know, everything. And you know, I was not to support them. What are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, listen. I need some money. I mean, she gave me five [INAUDIBLE] to take this one to the hospital because she has take the child to the hospital. She can't carry the child and the suitcase. I mean, I had no idea what it is. And besides, I have nothing, really. I mean, I was half-naked, and I'm-- I'm cold. And he should know what-- what the situation I-- I came from. I changed the whole-- whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: Story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of inquisiting me, I was inquisiting him. Anyhow, to end up we went to the main-- you know, where the trams are going. He was convinced that I am [INAUDIBLE]. And I am what I am. He gave me a few advices. I said, your coat is very shabby. There are so many things [INAUDIBLE]. So he gave me an address where I can go, where-- which things were taken away from Jews. I can get-- they dress me up. They give me everything. And that suitcase which I carry, I should never give it back to the bloody Jewish [INAUDIBLE]. Keep it yourself. And now you know. But if I see you again in this area doing this, I will shoot you on the spot. Heil Hitler. I went on the tram, and I was free. It took me hours. Really, I-- mentally I collapsed when I went to my sister's place and I saw my mother and Frank there. Then I know [? that we ?] actually [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told them my plan, what I'm doing. I'm going to take them to my place, where we live. But Frank looked at that time, according to Mother, sort of more Jewish sort of features. Maybe the ear were a little bit [? too long. ?] So my mother covered so many-- so much things up on Frank, that-- except for eyes. Nothing can be seen. And mother could fit in very well because my mother was not-- not hair and blue eyes. So she did not look Jewish. I mean, you know, she-- she could pass it. So I took them to my place, and I introduced Mother-- my mother to the-- whom I was sharing the flat. They were very happy. They showed everything, what to do, how to do. And the three of us slept in one room because there was no room for all of us. I mean, she's my auntie. And my-- and then my mother used that-- that documentation which she got with that girl's mother was whom we bought the papers from. Edith's-- Edith's so-called mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] just it was very difficult. Because mother [INAUDIBLE]. She was so strong, the Jewishness in her. I mean, she never ate anything which wasn't kosher. And she had to start to cook pork, whatever it was. But I always think I do it, this is for all-- existing for all life. The crunch of the matter came Friday night. I didn't know to lit candle or some-- very [? early ?] candle. I said, I saw two candles in the kitchen. So she took the two candles, put it in our room, locked the room, and she lit the candle, Shabbos candle on Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she had to light it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had to light it. She could not resist it. I mean, there's-- and-- so, you know, after this, she told me, said, my son, I can't make that-- can't live like that. Please, take me to-- to the ghetto. That time was already-- the ghetto was closed all that area. If God wants us to survive, we will survive. But this way, I will just drag you in. Yourself, too, and myself, and everything. I can't take it. Because the news came also-- we became suspicious and [INAUDIBLE]. Because [INAUDIBLE] it was house after house, they've been sort of-- came and-- and checked who is hiding, who is not hiding. So I took them back to the ghetto. I was just praying, you know, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not you, just the two of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the two. I mean, I-- I was all right. So I just tell them, you know, that the child is very sick. They had to go back to hospital. All these stories, you know, [INAUDIBLE] so my mother can stay because Frank is very sick. So they went to the-- went to the-- went to the ghetto. And that time, I could not go anymore into that house, because it was already sort of closed on. Ironically, ghetto was the safest place in the last few weeks in Budapest. Because I mean, taking Jews from hiding and-- and killing them. And also, from the protected houses. And for some reason, I don't know why, some of them left the-- left the ghetto on its own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even the men?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, there was not much men left, only older-- elder people. Men were everywhere, but not-- not there. So that's that was the part which [INAUDIBLE]. Then, yeah, I had a problem coming after that because the [INAUDIBLE] was-- you know, for year-- it was only 1925. But then they started to reduce. They called up, was 1926. So I had to be called up again. And so-- in the-- in the restaurant-- in that restaurant, I was working all the time. Mind you, I was upgraded to become a waiter after because all the men were called in, and they still needed somebody. So I was good enough for a waiter then. And I was in fairly good terms with the chef. The chef was a lady chef, a big woman. And she took liking on me when I was waiting. I mean, there was so many things scarce, but I actually get a lot of sort of tips she gave me. To my customers, she gave me always something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the work, you know, we used to sit together. And then she asked me. Said, you look very sad. What's-- what's wrong with you? I say, of course I am sad. I don't know what to do. I'm called up to the army. I mean, you know, said I was born in-- which was basically-- I wasn't called up, but I was in that age. I had to go. And-- and-- and sort of to this point, I mean, to-- to-- I said-- she said, you are not going to the army. You're going to see your mother. A mother has got a son like you can't [INAUDIBLE]. I will make everything [INAUDIBLE]. I said, but how? I say, I have to hide. So she lived not very far from the town hall square in Budapest. Right. She had a flat. I said, you come to my place. So I came to her place. I mean, I-- I didn't have very much to carry. And she put me up. She lived on the third floor. She put me up in a bed. The whole bed was made up. And I lie in it. And it could be covered up like nobody's in the bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she prepared some food, some drinks for me, a [? night pot ?] and everything. And you just stay there. And that's where you're going to be. And nobody will come around to this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't-- and did you still go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I mean, I just disappear-- I had to go to the army. That's what they knew. 1926. I mean, that-- that's the thing to do, to go to the army, because they needed every able-bodied.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then eventually, everything closed down, because it was coming very close to the end of the war. I mean, Budapest was encircled, and it was bombed and blasted. And-- and we lived in a second floor and the first floor is gone, and the third floor is gone. So I can't stay in a second floor up there because-- I mean, I'm not a Jew, but I will be killed [INAUDIBLE] of the bombing. So I had to go to the shelter down there. So one day, I mean, I didn't [? stay. ?] We had to go to the shelter. She-- she-- she took me down to the shelter and she introduced as [INAUDIBLE]. I mean, who I was. I was working in the same place. And she lived here. And have sort of a-- she had already-- because she lived there, she had allocated space. But there was no space for me. So in that house, there was a-- worked a madame, you know, who was providing these two beautiful girls. I mean, she was the widow of a high-ranking Hungarian officer. And obviously, that was sort of a part of the service which she were selling. And she kept the two girl. And so one of the girls-- I mean, I was 17 at the time. I was 19, I think. Yes. Said, [? Yanaka, ?] they called me. You join me in my place. I-- I share my bed with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I came to a point, you know, that I can't [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] Because my evidence was whatever happens, I deny. I am not Jewish. So, you know, I-- I blushed and [INAUDIBLE]. No, no, no. I wouldn't do anything like that. So there was a young man. Because in that place where I was, they just found out there was a few deserters from the army as well. So it was sort of a [INAUDIBLE] place for me to be because I'm not-- I wasn't the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then she offered-- he offered, listen, I give the [INAUDIBLE] to him. I join you in the bed. So I had to be accommodated, so he went-- he shared the bed with that-- with that beautiful girl. So that she shouldn't know that you were circumcised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I had. So we settle. And that place became quite a lively place for us. I mean, later that madame, when it was [INAUDIBLE]. She lived on the ground floor. So she went into her place and she called me. She would like to talk to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in and I said, listen, [INAUDIBLE]. You can't tell anyone, but you can tell me. You are a nice Jewish boy. And I said, I'm not Jewish. I deny to the last one. And she just said, whatever. You are-- I mean, your mannerisms, everything. I mean, I know people better than-- than any of them. So I think she knew it, but she did another thing for me after. But you-- you denied it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I denied it. I never admitted it to anyone. This was no-- no-- never. I mean, you know, this was-- so she-- when we went back to the shelter, she showed me that there is a secret outlet from the shelter. And it's not only for us-- for me, but, there are three deserters the army. So anything can happen, you disappear behind that secret shelter, so you will be safe. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fantastic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But-- I never-- I will tell you after what happened. So that place, because it was-- so the army was-- German army was [INAUDIBLE] closer and closer. And because of these two girls, they've been entertaining German soldiers. So with that entertainment of German soldiers, they always brought champagne and everything. I had such a sort of a life. I remember New Year's Eve 1944 to '45, we had champagne and caviar. I mean, it's not that I really missed it. But-- and became friends of these Germans. It's amazing. Because I spoke German. And they saw that I'm a Volksdeutsche. And anybody who spoke German, I mean, there's sort of a-- it was a comfort for them that they can at least communicate with someone. And I became their friends. So each time there was something, they brought me something special. When they sort of-- and-- one evening-- You see, there was a martial law that time in Budapest. Very strict one. No drinking. No alcohol. No [INAUDIBLE]. For everybody. Not for the Jews, because that was-- and they came in to celebrate with the two girls as it was a lull-- lull evening. So the caretaker's flat was still reasonable condition. So they went into the caretaker's flat, into one room. Wine drinking and singing. And while it was all [INAUDIBLE]. So I came up with those three--

speaker: interviewer
text: Deserters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we settled ourselves in the kitchen, kitchen table, and take out cards. And we start to play cards. Bang on the door later [INAUDIBLE]. So there was a [INAUDIBLE] guard, which was the [INAUDIBLE] part of the Hungary patrol hearing the music and voices as they came in, and they wanted to check what's happening. After all, it's martial law. How can they [? see? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: They came into the kitchen, and one Jewish boy and the other, you know, is sitting at the table. This-- this-- this should not be there. And we have to do something about it. And I mean, we all would sort of-- would have been executed on the spot. So the leader of the [? things, ?] he asked me, what that music and-- and singing? I said, they are our German brothers. So before he went into [INAUDIBLE], I would like to see them. Before he went in, I met one of the Germans who was quite friend-- a good friend of mine and tell him in German, listen, my friend. They are after these-- you and everything. I mean, I would suggest that you take-- take a glass of-- and a bottle of wine and give that bottle of wine to the leader and ask him to drink and not to-- not to bother us. And he was a little bit tipsy and he listened to every word what I said. So the fellow came in. You know, It's Heil Hitler, and everything. [INAUDIBLE] He was stunned. The German said, come here. So he went there. I mean, the German was still the rulers of the place. And gave him a glass of wine to drink. Oh, no. I can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Drink or I'll shoot your head apart. So he had no choice. I mean, he drank the wine. When he drank the wine, he became part of the [? culprit ?] as well. So he just saluted, Heil Hitler, and everything. And they walked out. I mean, I'm-- I'm I'm still around. I see, each sort of a thing, each sort of [INAUDIBLE]. I mean, I don't know what it really was. But I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like one miracle after--

speaker: interviewee
text: I always find, you know, somehow confidence. That-- I never sort of curled up in a corner and-- and-- and wait what's going to happen. So I was always one step-- one step ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, this place where we-- where we lived, it became quite sort of disturbing in so many ways. Because those Jews who were caught hiding and everything, on the end they brought all of them into that town hall square, which was quite famous later. And every night at 10 o'clock, machine gun shooting. They shot by hundreds, hundreds and thousands and thousands of Jews. I heard it, but I couldn't do anything. What can I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And just-- just to summarize these things, I don't think that in Budapest-- I don't know about Hungary-- one Jew was killed by a German soldier. Not one of them. They all were killed by a Hungarian [INAUDIBLE] guard [INAUDIBLE] without any-- without any mercy. It was dramatic. The German hadn't do the job. The Hungarians did everything. To the last minute, the one day before the Russians came into the area, they were still shooting. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the German came-- the-- the German left and the Russian came in. The first visitor I have was Edith. I think they came half an hour earlier there because we lived in a close vicinity. Was that the-- the point of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the point--

speaker: interviewee
text: Point of liberation, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. Nothing really happened in our house. It was already quite organized. I mean, we didn't have any sort of dramas, anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, we started to-- we still lived in a shelter because there was nothing which was [INAUDIBLE]. So Edith came. And I mentioned it's good to see her well and all right. And then we said, we have to find Mother and Frank. So we took off together. It was-- and to go towards the ghetto, it wasn't such a far away. Right? So we were walking towards the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we come close, there was that [? Dohan ?] synagogue, [? Dohan ?] temple. So, you know, it's-- it's-- and the gate of the-- of the-- of the ghetto was there. And from distance, we saw Frank and Mother are walking towards us. [SIGHS] No, it's just impossible. [LAUGHS] It is possible. Imagine that-- that meeting. I mean, this was-- to live this thing through, you know, it's-- it has got-- which so many people in the world, they will never have a chance. I wish they should not have the chance to live through this emotional sort of surprises and feelings. Losing and winning.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- so the four of us, we are-- we are alive. But how we looked, [INAUDIBLE]. We looked [? terrible. ?] But Mother and-- and Frank [INAUDIBLE]. So what do we do? Mother said, listen. You've got to go east. What's to go-- to go east? The Germans still can come back. You, know, because there was [INAUDIBLE]. So she could hardly walk. She was so-- so-- sort of tired. So we started to walk east. As we went east, how far can you go? I said, we can't go anywhere, really. So we went-- we went to [INAUDIBLE]. That was a nice sort of suburb.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have to tell you a story about that one as well. And we stayed there one night. There was-- it deceived us. Because there were people who are working when I was doing that pots and pan business who was very close associate. And when Mother and-- yeah. When Mother and Frank-- I took them out from that Swedish protected houses. And when-- sort of when Mother and Frank went back to ghetto, I had to leave that place as well. Because obviously, I become sort of a suspect. So when I leave-- left that place, I was nowhere to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, [INAUDIBLE]. It was reasonably cold. So I went to [INAUDIBLE] myself. And I went to that family, or to that-- which I was doing business with. And when the German came in, they ask us if we got anything to-- to-- to save or hide away. They will do everything for us. So we [? had ?] silverware, and radio, and everything with them. It was very close, sort of quite a friendship. And-- and-- so I came to them. And I told them, I have a problem. I have nowhere to stay. I have nowhere to live. Can you accommodate me for one night? It's funny. He said, you know, I mean, such a good friends. And they're sort of-- I say, I promise you, it's only one night. I won't cause you any trouble. They say, the trouble is not you, but the trouble is our son. I said, what's your son? The son is [INAUDIBLE]. And also, we are parents. Young people do [? this. ?] We couldn't prevent him. And if he comes home, because that was his room and bed. But on the end, I stayed overnight there. OK.
